subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The chapter's entire pedagogical architecture is built on applying deductive logic to prove number-theoretic statements. Section 5.1 presents three independent algebraic proofs of parity invariance and a systematic 'Always, Sometimes, or Never' framework requiring students to construct algebraic justifications for five divisibility claims; section 5.2 derives the digit-sum rules for 9, 3, and 11 from first principles using place-value algebra, producing theorems rather than just procedures.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Sections 5.1 and 5.2 consistently begin with pattern observation — sums of consecutive numbers, parities of all eight expressions formed from four numbers, remainders of powers of 10 when divided by 9 and 11, and digital root sequences — before algebraic generalisation, directly engaging the competency of discovering and describing rules for number pattern formation.

Section 5.3 requires students to develop their own solution strategies for cryptarithms (multiplication and addition variants), using algebraic constraints on place values and digit properties rather than following a prescribed algorithm; the worked examples model a reasoning process, not a template.

The chapter's explicit three-explanation treatment of parity invariance (Explanations 1, 2, 3) and its two-method treatment of the divisibility-by-11 shortcut directly model appreciating multiple solution paths; Math Talk prompts throughout ask students to share and compare their reasoning with classmates.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to reason about and prove properties of natural numbers by applying deductive logic, algebraic representation, and visualisation to derive and justify divisibility rules, parity invariance, and remainder characterisations.
